title: Piano Sonata No. 16 in C Major, K. 545, I. Allegro

musicalLanguage.description: This transcription of Mozart's Piano Sonata No. 16 in C Major, K. 545 (the first movement Allegro Mozart himself called a sonata for beginners) runs 146 measures at 137 BPM in 4/4 time with 2,714 notes, and the stated C major is strongly confirmed by the content (statedKeyFit 0.92; the detected key matches exactly). The form is sonata form with both halves marked to repeat, and the analysis proves it directly: measures 1-28 (the exposition) are byte-identical to measures 29-56 in the right hand and left hand alike, and the much longer span from measure 57 to measure 101 (development and recapitulation together) is likewise byte-identical to measures 102-146 — two full written-out repeats confirmed measure by measure across dozens of matching pairs. The chord windows trace a clean, mostly diatonic story: G major (the dominant) is the most-cited label at 16 windows (10% of measures considered), C major (the tonic) follows at 12 windows, with F major (the subdominant), Am, and Dm rounding out the progression.
musicalLanguage.structure: Sixteen structural regions from the section-lens pass, evenly spaced (mostly 6-14 measures each) in keeping with a piece built from clear, balanced phrases. The exposition spans measures 1-28 and repeats verbatim at measures 29-56 — a 292-interval right-hand span and a 71-interval left-hand span both confirmed as exact, untransposed matches. The development and recapitulation together span measures 57-101 and repeat verbatim at measures 102-146 (a 158-interval left-hand span and a massive 547-interval right-hand span, both exact matches) — together the two repeated halves account for the great majority of the piece's total length. Inside the recapitulation block, the famous scalar right-hand passagework (measures 63-68, restating at 108-113) alternates with the Alberti-bass left hand, and the busiest measures in the piece — 98 and 143, each 40 combined onsets, mirroring each other exactly across the two halves — mark the technical peak both times it occurs.
keyMoments: The exposition (measures 1-28) is byte-identical to its repeat at measures 29-56 in both hands, and the development-recapitulation block (measures 57-101) is likewise byte-identical to measures 102-146 — this teaches sonata form's most basic structural lesson directly from the data: both large halves really were meant to be played twice.

G major (the dominant) is cited more often than C major (the tonic) across the whole piece — 16 windows to 12 — which demonstrates how a Classical-era exposition spends much of its time establishing and confirming the dominant key before a Classical era recapitulation restores the tonic.

Measures 98 and 143 are the two busiest in the piece, each carrying exactly 40 combined onsets, and each sits at the same position within its own repeated half — proof that Mozart placed the technical peak at a matching structural point both times, not by accident.

The scalar right-hand passagework at measures 63-68 (which restates identically at measures 108-113) demonstrates even scale technique across an octave and more — a foundational skill this famously pedagogical sonata was written to teach.
teachingGoals: Learn the exposition (measures 1-28) once and recognize that its repeat at measures 29-56 is genuinely note-for-note — this halves the real learning work despite the score's length.

Practice the scalar passagework at measures 63-68 (and its identical return at measures 108-113) hands separately first, since even scale technique under the Alberti bass is the core skill this movement teaches.

Listen for the shift in emphasis from G major (the dominant, 16 windows) toward C major (the tonic, 12 windows) across the piece, and develop the ear to recognize when a Classical-era piece is confirming its home key versus its dominant.

Build stamina for the densest measures (98 and 143, 40 onsets each) by practicing them slowly first — recognizing that both are the technical peak of their respective repeated half helps target practice time efficiently.
styleTips: Keep the Alberti bass light and perfectly even under the right hand — listen for the left hand disappearing into the texture while the melody stays in front.
Use clean, minimal pedal — Classical-era articulation depends on clarity; listen for each note in the scalar passagework at measures 63-68 (and its twin at 108-113) speaking distinctly.

Play the dominant-leaning material (the G major windows) with a touch more forward energy than the tonic-grounded passages — listen for the harmonic pull toward G resolving satisfyingly when C major returns.
Shape phrases with an elegant, vocal quality even in fast passagework — listen for the densest measures (98 and 143) staying musical and shaped rather than turning into pure technical display.
